input.instructions: The problem asks for a reconstruction of a sequence of sibling-merge operations that transforms an initial rooted tree into a final rooted tree. Core Algorithmic Blueprint: 1. State Representation: Represent the trees using adjacency lists or parent-child structures. The key is to identify which nodes are siblings in the initial tree and how they are merged into a single child in the final tree. 2. Mathematical Invariants: A sibling-merge operation reduces the number of children of a parent node by k-1 when k siblings are merged into one. The total number of nodes remains constant, but the degree of certain nodes decreases. 3. Constructive Logic (Bottom-Up/Top-Down): Identify the target structure. Since we are merging siblings, we should look at the children of each node in the final tree. For each node u in the final tree, its children v1, v2, ..., vm must have originated from a set of siblings in the initial tree. Use a post-order traversal (bottom-up) or a topological approach to ensure that when we merge siblings, we do so in an order that does not violate the tree structure. 4. Algorithm Steps: Map the final tree structure back to the initial tree's nodes. For every node in the final tree, identify the group of siblings in the initial tree that were merged to form its child. Generate the sequence of operations: For a group of k siblings {s1, s2, ..., sk} being merged into a single node snew, the operations are (s1, s2), (snew, s3), ..., (snew, sk) or similar, depending on the specific merge rule. Ensure the sequence respects the dependency: a node can only be merged once it is ready. 5. Complexity Goal: The solution should be O(N) or O(N log N) to handle the constraints of a Gold-level USACO problem.
